channel.title: Drupal CMS
channel.description: Stay informed about developments in Drupal CMS.

item.title: #3577804: The installer should set a state flag to remember what site template was applied
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3577804 · 1 contributor · 2 comments
  • Followers: 1

Summary

During Drupal CMS installation, when a user selects the "Blank" (starter kit) site template on the site template selection form, the installer now stores the chosen template's recipe locator in the site_template_base state key. This state flag is deliberately environment-local (state is never ported between environments) and is used by a new export endpoint that lets users with the administer site configuration permission download the current site as a recipe ZIP file from /admin/config/development/site-export. This lays the groundwork for future contextual guidance in the admin UI. This change was implemented as part of #3575283.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New @api route /admin/config/development/site-export for downloading the current site as a recipe ZIP, and site_template_base state key written during installation when the 'Blank' site template is selected.

Technical details

In SiteTemplateForm::submitForm(), a new StateInterface dependency is injected. When the submitted choice is drupal_cms_site_template_base, the recipe locator path is written to state via $this->state->set('site_template_base', $locator) before the recipe is enqueued. Only the "Blank" starter kit triggers this; other site templates (Haven, Byte, etc.) do not set the key.

The state key feeds the new ExportController::exportArchive() (an @internal controller in drupal_cms_helper), which calls SiteExporter::export($destination, $this->state()->get('site_template_base')). SiteExporter::export() was refactored to accept an optional ?string $base parameter: when a base path is provided and the directory exists, the new private copyBaseRecipe() method mirrors it to the destination (excluding content/) and renames any *.example files by stripping the suffix. When no base is set, the directory is prepared from scratch as before. The exporter also now deletes config/.htaccess from the output, since the exported recipe is meant to be shared.

A new route drupal_cms_helper.site_export at /admin/config/development/site-export is marked # @api in drupal_cms_helper.routing.yml. It requires the administer site configuration permission and a custom exportArchiveAccess() check that verifies PharData is available. The ExportController class itself carries the @internal marker; only the route path is part of the public API surface.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: March 8, 2026

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3542339: Make the blank site option usable
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3542339 · 1 contributor · 2 comments
  • Followers: 2

Summary

When choosing the blank site option in Drupal CMS (via the drupal_cms_starter recipe), a site previously installed with only a 404 page and no real starting point. This has been fixed: the recipe now provisions a proper "Home" canvas page at /home as the front page, along with configured header and footer regions using the Mercury theme's Navbar and Footer components.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New Canvas component configurations for sdc.mercury.button, sdc.mercury.footer, sdc.mercury.hero-billboard, and sdc.mercury.navbar are now shipped by the drupal_cms_starter recipe

Technical details

The fix (landed in #3558958, MR !688) adds several things to the drupal_cms_starter recipe. New Canvas component configuration YAML files register four Mercury theme SDC components: sdc.mercury.button, sdc.mercury.footer, sdc.mercury.hero-billboard, and sdc.mercury.navbar. Two new Canvas layout configs, mercury.header and mercury.footer, wire up the Mercury Navbar (with site branding block and main navigation menu) and Mercury Footer (with footer menu block) as the default header and footer regions.

The old default content, a canvas_page entity titled "Page not found" at /404, is replaced by a new canvas_page entity titled "Home" at /home. The Home page uses a sdc.mercury.hero-billboard layout component as its root, with a Heading ("Your content goes here"), a Text block ("Bring your site to life, starting here."), and a Button ("Call to action") nested inside its hero_slot. The recipe action that previously set system.site page.404 to /404 is replaced by one that sets page.front to /home, making the new page the site's front page.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: August 21, 2025

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3526844: [meta] Implement the first real-world site template
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +23/−0 · yml +14 · json +9
  • Discussion: #3526844 · 6 contributors · 23 comments
  • Followers: 17

Summary

Drupal CMS is shipping its first real-world site template, aimed at SaaS and B2B product marketing websites. The template bundles an opinionated set of recipes, content types, layout components, and demo content so a site owner can spin up a product site with a blog, feature highlights, pricing section, FAQ, testimonials, and a contact form. The implementation ultimately landed as the separate byte project (drupal.org/project/byte) rather than inside the main Drupal CMS repository.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New drupal_cms_b2b_template site recipe (now the byte project at drupal.org/project/byte) for bootstrapping SaaS and B2B product marketing sites on Drupal CMS.

Technical details

The MR scaffolded a drupal/drupal_cms_b2b_template recipe (type: Site) requiring drupal/core ^11.2 and drupal/drupal_cms_blog ^2. The recipe.yml applied administrator_role, content_editor_role, and drupal_cms_blog as recipe dependencies, and imported system.menu.footer and system.menu.main config. The MR was closed without merging; the full implementation moved to the byte project.

The issue body defines the intended content model: Blog Post (delegated to drupal_cms_blog as a dependency rather than copied), Feature and FAQ content types as nodes (so editors find all content at /admin/content), and a Changelog page built on drupal_cms_page. Layout components (Hero, Features, Pricing, Logo grid, Testimonial list, CTA banner) are planned as SDCs, pending the new Drupal CMS design system. Contact form support comes from the existing drupal_cms_forms recipe. A Marketing role is added alongside the standard administrator and editor roles from core recipes.

Microcontent reusability (testimonials, FAQs as shared entities) was debated and explicitly excluded from MVP: pameeela noted the reuse case does not meet the 80% threshold. Behavior tracking, CRM integration, and carousels are deferred to future issues. The dependency on the entity_display_route contrib module was raised (by thejimbirch) since it lacks a stable release and security coverage; the canonical fix is tracked in Drupal core issue #3483304.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: May 27, 2025

Collaboration

pameeela removed the microcontent heading from the issue summary after deciding that making testimonials and FAQs globally reusable entities adds complexity without meeting the 80%-use-case threshold for MVP. That single judgment call settled the content model: everything stays as plain nodes. catch pushed back early on whether drupal_cms_blog would be copied or reused as a dependency; phenaproxima confirmed it is a declared recipe dependency, establishing the pattern for the whole template.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3501978: Direct links to flush caches, run cron, and such
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3501978 · 3 contributors · 4 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Drupal CMS now includes the navigation_extra_tools contributed module by default. This gives all Drupal CMS sites one-click links in the navigation toolbar to flush all caches, run cron, run database updates, and other common administrative operations. Previously, site builders had to navigate to Configuration > Development > Performance to clear caches, or install Admin Toolbar Extra Tools separately to get these shortcuts.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: New navigation_extra_tools module bundled in the base Drupal CMS recipe, providing toolbar shortcuts to flush caches, run cron, and run updates on all Drupal CMS sites

Technical details

The navigation_extra_tools module (version ^1.3) was added to the install list in recipes/drupal_cms_site_template_base/recipe.yml and declared as a Composer dependency in recipes/drupal_cms_site_template_base/composer.json. Because drupal_cms_site_template_base is the foundational recipe applied to every Drupal CMS installation, these toolbar shortcuts are available on all Drupal CMS sites without any additional setup. The module integrates with core's Navigation module, which Drupal CMS already installs. The feature request was prompted by the observation that Drupal CMS does not assume Admin Toolbar is installed, leaving users without the quick-access admin links they were accustomed to from Admin Toolbar Extra Tools.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 24, 2025

Collaboration

The reporter, chike, noted that after switching to Drupal CMS they repeatedly had to navigate to Configuration > Development > Performance to flush caches because the familiar Admin Toolbar shortcuts were absent. A follow-up comment from thejimbirch pointed to the navigation_extra_tools module as the right solution. chike then specifically called for it to be bundled, describing it as "absolutely essential," which is the direct path to the fix recorded here.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3501076: Remove search_api_autocomplete until performance can be improved
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal unknown (0)
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 3 files, +0/−52 · yml +0/−51 · json +0/−1
  • Discussion: #3501076 · 3 contributors · 9 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

The Drupal CMS search recipe previously required search_api_autocomplete, which loaded approximately 280kb of JavaScript for anonymous users. This alone caused the Lighthouse performance score to drop from 95 to 86 after applying the search recipe. The proposed change removes search_api_autocomplete from the recipe entirely, eliminating that JavaScript payload and the associated Lighthouse regression. Autocomplete may be reintroduced later if a low-cost approach is found (see #3500964 for the upstream performance work).

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The diff removes every trace of search_api_autocomplete from the drupal_cms_search recipe. Concretely: the search_api_autocomplete.search.content_autocomplete config entity file is deleted; drupal/search_api_autocomplete is dropped from composer.json; search_api_autocomplete is removed from the recipe's module install list; the simple_search_form block config for both the desktop and mobile form instances has its input_type: search_api_autocomplete setting and the entire nested search_api_autocomplete config block removed; and the grantPermission: 'use search_api_autocomplete for content_autocomplete' actions for both user.role.anonymous and user.role.authenticated are removed.

The maintainers noted that removing the module also requires cleaning up any autocomplete-specific theming in drupal_cms_olivero (tracked in #3501069), because the search form falls back to a broken state without additional theme adjustments. Those theme changes are considered out of scope for this MR and would be addressed separately.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 21, 2025

Collaboration

pameeela weighed in with a clear trade-off analysis: the value of autocomplete suggesting individual pages was not worth a nine-point Lighthouse regression, and splitting it into a separate recipe would not help because the performance cost alone makes it unsuitable as a default. That framing helped settle the direction toward removal rather than extraction.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3501069: Search broken without search_api_autocomplete
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3501069 · 2 contributors · 5 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

When a site admin uninstalled search_api_autocomplete from a Drupal CMS site that had the Search recipe applied, the site's search stopped working entirely. The Olivero theme's search-wide.js threw an uncaught TypeError trying to call .focus() on a null element, the search dropdown rendered incorrectly, and clicking the search field immediately redirected to the search results page rather than letting the user type. The Search recipe now no longer requires search_api_autocomplete or simple_search_form. Uninstalling those modules will not break search on sites running Drupal CMS.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The original drupal_cms_search recipe (added in #3468271) configured a simple_search_form block with input_type: search_api_autocomplete, pointing at the content_autocomplete search configuration. Olivero's search-wide.js assumed this autocomplete widget was always present in the DOM; its handleFocus handler at line 86 called .focus() on the autocomplete input element unconditionally, producing Cannot read properties of null (reading 'focus') when the module was absent. The dependency was hard-coded with no fallback, making Drupal CMS incompatible with any search_api + simple_search_form setup that omits search_api_autocomplete.

The fix, delivered through issue #3567584, replaced the entire search form implementation. search_api_autocomplete and simple_search_form were removed from the recipe's install list and composer.json requirements. In their place, canvas, canvas_stark, eca_config, and eca_user were added. The search Views display was converted from a routed page at /search to a block display (views_block:search-block) intended to be placed via Canvas. An ECA model (eca.eca.search_exclude.yml) now handles automatically enabling search_api_exclude for new content types as they are created. The permission grants for use search_api_autocomplete for content_autocomplete on the anonymous and authenticated roles were also removed.

Upgrade

Existing sites that already applied drupal_cms_search retain their previously installed modules and configuration. Re-applying the updated recipe will not automatically remove search_api_autocomplete or simple_search_form; those must be uninstalled manually if desired. New applications of the recipe will install Canvas and ECA instead, and the search form is rendered as a block rather than appearing at /search. The anonymous and authenticated user permissions for use search_api_autocomplete for content_autocomplete are no longer granted by the recipe.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 21, 2025

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3499391: Composer gets mad because ExperienceBuilderDemo.php is a symlink
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Critical bug report
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +7/−5 · json +7/−4 · php +0/−1
  • Discussion: #3499391 · 5 contributors · 19 comments
  • Followers: 9

Summary

On the Drupal CMS 1.x development branch, developers who clone the repo and try to install additional modules via Project Browser hit a hard Composer error: "Could not scan for classes inside 'ExperienceBuilderDemo.php' which does not appear to be a file nor a folder." The cause is a symlink named ExperienceBuilderDemo.php at the project root that Composer's autoloader cannot handle. The proposed fix removes that symlink and registers the real file via autoload.classmap in composer.json, while also moving cweagans/composer-patches to require-dev and loosening the drupal/default_content constraint to ^2@alpha. The MR was ultimately closed without merging after the pipeline failed; catch opened a separate follow-up, #3501190, to remove the demo script from the project template entirely.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root of the problem is that ExperienceBuilderDemo.php at the project root is a symlink pointing to project_template/ExperienceBuilderDemo.php. When Composer resolves the autoload configuration it follows the project root, encounters the symlink, and cannot classify it as a file or directory, causing the scan to abort. The proposed MR removes the symlink (tracked in version control as a single-line file containing its target path) and adds an explicit autoload.classmap entry in composer.json pointing directly to project_template/ExperienceBuilderDemo.php, bypassing symlink resolution entirely. The same diff moves cweagans/composer-patches from require to require-dev, so the patching plugin is no longer installed on production sites, and tightens the drupal/default_content constraint to ^2@alpha to allow alpha releases. phenaproxima noted after the MR opened that the approach still caused pipeline failures, leaving the issue unresolved by this specific change.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 13, 2025

Collaboration

catch pushed repeatedly for removing Experience Builder and the demo script from the 1.x branch altogether rather than patching around the symlink, arguing that a script with no upgrade path would be unremovable for potentially tens of thousands of site owners once it shipped in a release. phenaproxima defended the targeted fix as the narrowest change needed to unblock development, while agreeing the long-term answer was moving the script into Experience Builder itself. The disagreement remained unresolved in this issue; catch opened #3501190 to pursue full removal separately.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3497858: [PP1] Add Google Analytics recipe to Project Browser allowed list has been updated.
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3497858 · 2 contributors · 2 comments
  • Followers: 2

Summary

Drupal CMS site owners can now install the Google Analytics recipe directly through the Project Browser UI. Previously, the recipe was hidden from Project Browser because it required interactive inputs (a Google Tag Manager ID) that Project Browser could not prompt for. With alpha7 of Project Browser adding recipe input support, drupal_cms_google_analytics was added to the allowed recipes list alongside drupal_cms_ai and drupal_cms_accessibility_tools. Project Browser was also configured to allow only one recipe selection at a time (max_selections: 1).

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Three Drupal CMS recipes (drupal_cms_google_analytics, drupal_cms_ai, drupal_cms_accessibility_tools) are now listed in the Project Browser allowed set and installable via the UI

Technical details

The change lands in the drupal_cms_starter recipe's recipe.yml. The project_browser.admin_settings config block's allowed_projects.recipes list received three new entries: drupal_cms_google_analytics, drupal_cms_ai, and drupal_cms_accessibility_tools. At the same time, max_selections: 1 was added to that same config block, and the drupal/project_browser dependency in drupal_cms_starter's composer.json was bumped from ^2-alpha6 to ^2-alpha7. The same commit also updated AI-related module dependencies from pre-release beta constraints (^1-beta*) to stable ^1 constraints, and raised the project template's minimum-stability from alpha to beta. This was delivered as part of the broader issue #3498277, which phenaproxima described as waiting on the Project Browser alpha7 release before merging.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 7, 2025

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3497854: [PP1] Add AI recipe to Project Browser allowed list
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3497854 · 2 contributors · 5 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Drupal CMS now surfaces the drupal_cms_ai, drupal_cms_google_analytics, and drupal_cms_accessibility_tools recipes inside the Project Browser UI. Previously the AI recipe was intentionally hidden because the guided API-key setup flow was not yet ready; it could only be applied at the command line. With the setup UI now in place and AI modules reaching stable releases, all three recipes were added to the Project Browser allowed list alongside the existing content-type and tooling recipes. Project Browser itself was updated to alpha7, and a max_selections: 1 limit was set so users choose one recipe per session.

Impact

Affects module maintainers.

  • New API: Three new recipes (drupal_cms_ai, drupal_cms_google_analytics, drupal_cms_accessibility_tools) are now selectable in the Project Browser UI on Drupal CMS sites.

Technical details

The change is in drupal_cms_starter's recipe YAML, specifically the project_browser.admin_settings config block. The allowed_projects.recipes list was expanded to include drupal_cms_ai, drupal_cms_google_analytics, and drupal_cms_accessibility_tools, and sorted alphabetically. A new max_selections: 1 key was added to the same config block. The drupal/project_browser dependency constraint in drupal_cms_starter's composer.json moved from ^2-alpha6 to ^2-alpha7. Separately, the AI module version constraints in drupal_cms_ai's composer.json were promoted from beta pins (^1-beta7, ^1-beta1, etc.) to stable ^1 ranges for drupal/ai, drupal/ai_agents, drupal/ai_image_alt_text, drupal/ai_provider_anthropic, and drupal/ai_provider_openai. Two YAML trailing-comma syntax fixes in the AI recipe's input configuration and a scheduler constraint bump (^2 to ^2.2) were also included. The project template's minimum-stability was raised from alpha to beta.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 7, 2025

Collaboration

The merge request sparked a substantive debate about readiness. poker10 raised concerns about exposing the AI recipe given quality signals: several AI modules had fewer than 100 installs and lacked security advisory coverage, and early testing revealed hallucination, broken links, and Guzzle timeout issues. pameeela explained the recipe had been hidden specifically because the guided API-key setup UI was not ready, not because of quality concerns, and that the AI team had conducted end-user testing in coordination with UX; the AI modules were tagging stable releases before the merge. The discussion clarified the deliberate product decision to proceed, with the practical constraint that users still need a paid API key from OpenAI or Anthropic to get anything working.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3493438: Klaro significantly adds to anonymous visitor page weight
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal unknown (0)
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3493438 · 4 contributors · 10 comments
  • Followers: 9

Summary

Out of the box, Drupal CMS no longer loads any Klaro JavaScript or CSS for anonymous visitors. Previously the Klaro consent management library (klaro-no-css.js) accounted for more than 90% of the JavaScript served to anonymous users, at 212 KB uncompressed (65 KB compressed). The fix ensures Klaro assets are only loaded when at least one tracked service (such as Google Analytics) is actually activated in the site's configuration. Additionally, the Klaro library itself has been rebuilt: the file was switched from klaro-no-css.js to klaro-no-translations-no-css.js and recompiled targeting Drupal's browser compatibility defaults rather than very old browsers, reducing it from ~208 KB to ~64 KB when services are active.

Impact

Affects site owners.

  • Config change: Klaro JS/CSS only loads on pages where at least one consent-managed service is activated; a default Drupal CMS install with no third-party services enabled sends no Klaro assets to anonymous visitors.

Technical details

The issue was tracked at the Drupal CMS level and resolved through fixes in several places. Issue #3493540 tightened ECA (Event-Condition-Action) configuration so that Klaro JavaScript files are only added to the page when services are actually activated, bringing the default anonymous page to zero Klaro assets. Issue #3491681 (Klaro module) swapped the bundled asset from klaro-no-css.js to klaro-no-translations-no-css.js and recompiled the library against Drupal's baseline browser targets rather than broad legacy-browser polyfill targets, shrinking the asset from ~208 KB to ~64 KB.

A secondary performance symptom noted by catch was that Klaro's hook_js_settings_alter() implementation injects a large amount of data into JS settings on every page request, costing ~16 ms even on render-cached pages. This cost is now avoided on pages where no services are active.

Issue #3494580 updated Drupal CMS performance tests to assert that a default (no services enabled) anonymous front page loads zero bytes of Klaro JS and CSS.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 11, 2024

Collaboration

catch discovered this issue while profiling PHP performance: Klaro's hook_js_settings_alter() was taking ~16 ms on render-cached pages due to injecting a large settings payload, which led to checking the front-end where the library size became apparent. The maintainer of the COOKiES module (anybody) joined to clarify that switching to COOKiES was not the right path and that COOKiES is actually planned to be deprecated in favour of Klaro once feature parity and an upgrade path exist. jurgenhaas confirmed the architectural reasoning behind the Klaro choice (documented in the project's ADR) and agreed the library size needed to be addressed upstream rather than by switching tools.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3498068: Custom styling for home page
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3498068 · 3 contributors · 7 comments
  • Followers: 3

Summary

Drupal CMS's home page, which uses a basic page content type, received custom styling to set it apart visually from other basic pages. The key change is that the page title is kept in the DOM as an accessible h1 but hidden from sighted users, ensuring screen reader compatibility while matching the design intent of a title-free hero layout. The Drupal CMS Olivero sub-theme (drupal_cms_olivero) handles this with a PHP preprocess hook and a small CSS utility file.

Impact

Affects front-end developers.

  • Theme change: The drupal_cms_olivero sub-theme now visually hides the h1 page title on the front page via a preprocess hook and a new utility.css class rather than removing the block from the DOM.

Technical details

The original approach (issue #3491494, December 2024) used a request_path block visibility condition on block.block.drupal_cms_olivero_page_title to suppress the page title block entirely on the front page. This left the home page without any h1 element, which was an accessibility problem reported as issue #3500408 and fixed in the 1.0.1 release (MR !462).

The fix removes the request_path visibility config so the page title block renders everywhere. In drupal_cms_olivero_preprocess_block() in drupal_cms_olivero.theme, a call to \Drupal::service('path.matcher')->isFrontPage() checks whether the current page is the front page; if so, and the block plugin is page_title_block, the CSS class visually-hidden is appended to the block's attributes. A new css/base/utility.css file defines that class as width: 1px !important, with the !important required to override Olivero's own specificity on visually-hidden elements. That stylesheet is added to the global-styling library in drupal_cms_olivero.libraries.yml.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: January 8, 2025

Collaboration

kristen pol picked up the issue late one evening and worked through Olivero's layout CSS, including its wide-content.css and breakpoint variables, trying negative margins to shift content alignment. She was unable to get the approach stable across breakpoints and described the complexity to the thread. mherchel, who originally wrote the Olivero grid code (noting it was built for IE11 compatibility at the time), stepped in and confirmed he would look at it. The visible page title issue that emerged from the initial implementation was later resolved in issue #3500408.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3491177: Responsive Image Styles should not depend on a theme for its breakpoints
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 35 files, +169/−96 · yml +147/−96 · json +11 · php +11
  • Discussion: #3491177 · 4 contributors · 17 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

The drupal_cms_image recipe's responsive image styles were hardwired to the olivero breakpoint group, meaning Olivero had to remain installed and could not be uninstalled without breaking those styles. This was a problem for sites that wanted to evaluate other themes. MR !550 proposed fixing this by introducing a tiny, hidden theme called drupal_cms_breakpoints that owns only the breakpoints and nothing else, so the image recipe no longer depends on Olivero. The MR was closed without merging; the issue was ultimately marked fixed because Drupal CMS 2.x moved to an ECA-based approach that defines breakpoints in configuration instead, making them fully customizable and theme-independent.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

Every responsive image style config file in the drupal_cms_image recipe had breakpoint_group: olivero and breakpoint_id: olivero.* references, which forced Olivero to remain installed (Drupal's Breakpoint module only accepts breakpoints defined by modules or themes, not raw config).

The MR proposes a minimal hidden theme: two files (drupal_cms_breakpoints.info.yml with hidden: true and drupal_cms_breakpoints.breakpoints.yml) bundled inside recipes/drupal_cms_image/assets/. The drupal_cms_image recipe's composer.json declares Composer scaffold mappings that copy those two files into [web-root]/themes/drupal_cms_breakpoints/. The top-level composer.json is updated to add drupal/drupal_cms_image to allowed-packages in the scaffold config. The recipe's recipe.yml then installs drupal_cms_breakpoints instead of olivero. All breakpoint_group and breakpoint_id references across every responsive image style config are rewritten from olivero / olivero.sm / olivero.md / etc. to drupal_cms_breakpoints / drupal_cms_breakpoints.sm / etc.

The breakpoint values themselves (sm at 500 px, md at 700 px, lg at 1000 px, xl at 1300 px, plus nav and grid variants) are borrowed from Olivero, so the actual image rendering behaviour does not change; only the ownership of those breakpoint definitions moves to the new glue theme. A concern raised in the thread was that this scaffold approach ties the recipe to drupal/cms projects specifically, which prompted discussion of alternatives including reusing drupal_cms_olivero and waiting for the Mediacurrent design-system theme. The eventual fix in 2.x used an ECA model to store breakpoints in configuration rather than in any theme file.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: December 2, 2024

Collaboration

The design debate in this issue is worth noting. phenaproxima's comment (#16121341) proposed the scaffold-backed hidden theme as a creative workaround for Drupal core's strict rule that only modules and themes can define breakpoints. catch (#16121362) pushed back by suggesting that sizes-only responsive image styles could avoid the problem entirely and pointed to #3499319. tonypaulbarker (#16126386) explained why sizes alone would not work for art-direction use cases, keeping the breakpoint dependency in scope. phenaproxima (#16126418) then questioned his own MR, worrying the scaffold approach made the recipe incompatible with non-CMS Composer projects, and floated the idea of repurposing drupal_cms_olivero as a minimal breakpoint-only theme in a future 2.x branch. The thread concluded when phenaproxima reported (#16351570) that Drupal CMS 2.x had already solved this differently via an ECA model, making the MR moot.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3488359: Support retina devices and improve responsive image styles on mobile
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3488359 · 3 contributors · 5 comments
  • Followers: 6

Summary

Drupal CMS's responsive image system was previously tied to Olivero's breakpoints, which lack a sub-500px breakpoint and only define 1x screen resolutions. This caused the fallback image to be underutilised on small phones and images to appear blurry on high-density (retina) screens. The fix introduces a dedicated custom breakpoint group for Drupal CMS, decoupled from any theme, and updates all responsive image styles to use it. Sites using the drupal_cms_site_template_base recipe now serve appropriately sized images across a wider range of viewport widths and device pixel ratios.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The breakpoints are defined at runtime, not via a static .breakpoints.yml file, using an ECA model at eca.eca.define_breakpoints.yml in the drupal_cms_site_template_base recipe. The model listens to the eca_render:breakpoints_alter event and fires four sequential eca_render_alter_breakpoint actions to register custom.sm (min-width: 500px), custom.md (min-width: 700px), custom.lg (min-width: 1000px), and custom.xl (min-width: 1300px) under the custom breakpoint group. All responsive image styles in drupal_cms_site_template_base/config/ declare breakpoint_group: custom and use image_mapping_type: sizes rather than explicit 1x/2x multiplier mappings. The sizes approach emits a srcset with multiple candidate widths and a sizes attribute; browsers then pick the best candidate based on viewport width and device pixel ratio natively, covering retina screens without needing separate 1.5x or 2x image style entries. Viewports below 500px are handled by the fallback_image_style property defined on each responsive image style. The chosen approach (option 4 from the issue) keeps the breakpoints fully independent of the active front-end theme.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 19, 2024

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3488457: Ensure that Drupal CMS can be updated with automatic updates
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Critical unknown (0)
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3488457 · 1 contributor · 2 comments
  • Followers: 3

Summary

Drupal CMS has established a formal policy ensuring that stable releases (RC and beyond) never ship with patched dependencies. Because the automatic_updates module's ComposerPatchesValidator blocks updates when a patcher like cweagans/composer-patches is present, shipping patches in stable releases would silently break the ability for non-technical site owners to apply unattended updates. The fix separates development patching (allowed, for CI and internal work) from production releases (prohibited). Site owners can rely on automatic updates working out of the box on any stable Drupal CMS release.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The root problem is that package_manager's ComposerPatchesValidator requires the patcher to be a direct, explicitly managed dependency and blocks updates whenever a patch fails to apply or is ambiguous. If cweagans/composer-patches were part of the distributed project template, any recipe-applied patch could silently break update capability for non-developer site owners who did not knowingly install the patcher.

The implementation separates concerns across two composer manifests. The distributed project template (project_template/composer.json) contains no reference to cweagans/composer-patches. The internal development manifest (dev.composer.json) declares cweagans/composer-patches: ^2 under require-dev, keeping it entirely out of production installs. Development-time patches are collected in patches.json at the repo root, with a _readme key explicitly marking them as "strictly for internal development and CI." The patches/ directory holds the actual patch files (currently limited to drupal/core patches for Umami, date field default value, and PHPUnit recipe discovery).

CONTRIBUTING.md codifies the policy in prose: patches are permitted to assist internal development and CI, must never ship in stable releases, and any dependency that still requires a patch when an RC is tagged must be removed from Drupal CMS entirely. Acceptable exit strategies include securing a maintainer commitment backed by granting commit access to a Drupal CMS committer, removing the dependent feature, or polyfilling the functionality via a contributed module. The parallel issue #3488829 captured the governance discussion that led to this outcome.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 19, 2024

Collaboration

phenaproxima opened #3488829 as the governance counterpart to catch's bug report, proposing concrete patch lifecycle rules and several exit strategies for patches that do not get committed upstream in time. catch contributed the idea of using separate main and release branches (analogous to how core handles experimental modules) and noted that functionality can be extracted to an independent contrib project instead of remaining a patch. berdir raised a meaningful concern that some core issues take years to resolve, asked how the policy would handle long-lived patches, and linked to related policy issue #3491176 on the impact to contrib maintainers. phenaproxima responded with a ranked set of exit strategies matched to different patch scenarios (new non-BC features, bugfix dependencies, complex new features, and nice-to-haves). pameeela noted from experience that the team had already removed some features where patches were not reviewed in time, framing the loss as an acceptable outcome given the project's mission.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3487486: Add Events Calendar recipe
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 5 files, +444/−0 · yml +373 · md +59 · json +12
  • Discussion: #3487486 · 4 contributors · 5 comments
  • Followers: 5

Summary

This issue proposed bundling an Events Calendar recipe directly into Drupal CMS, adding a FullCalendar-based interactive calendar view for Event nodes. The feature was demonstrated at the Barcelona demo using a contrib recipe but was not yet included in the distribution. After discussion, maintainers decided against shipping the recipe as part of Drupal CMS itself, opting instead to add it as a Composer suggest entry so site owners can discover and apply it optionally. The full bundling proposal was closed in favour of a suggests-only approach tracked in #3487703.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The MR introduced a new drupal_cms_events_calendar recipe under the Drupal CMS project. The recipe declares drupal_cms_events as a required upstream recipe and installs the fullcalendar module (drupal/fullcalendar:^3.0). It ships a single Views configuration (events_calendar) using the fullcalendar style plugin, exposing title and field_when (via smartdate_default formatter) as fields. The view is configured as a page at events/calendar that renders as a navigation tab alongside the existing events views. The view header includes an add_content_by_bundle button labelled "Add an Event". The calendar is configured with day-grid, time-grid, and list sub-views, defaulting to dayGridMonth as the initial display.

The existing events recipe's composer.json gains a "suggest" key pointing to drupal/drupal_cms_events_calendar with a short description, so Composer can surface the option to site builders without requiring it automatically.

The MR was closed without merging after phenaproxima raised concerns about micro-recipe proliferation ("we need to trend away from micro-recipes for distribution and maintenance reasons") and pameeela noted that most sites do not want a monthly calendar OOTB. The suggest-only resolution keeps the calendar available as an opt-in add-on without bundling it into the core distribution.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 14, 2024

Collaboration

The thread surfaces a clear design tension that shaped the outcome. thejimbirch immediately raised a maintenance concern: the contrib events_calendar recipe already exists, so bundling a copy would mean maintaining the same thing in two places. phenaproxima broadened the objection to the general principle of micro-recipes, arguing it is much easier to split a large recipe later than to merge or eliminate micro-recipes once they exist. pameeela added a product argument: an interactive monthly calendar is niche enough that shipping it out of the box would surprise most users. Facing that consensus, mandclu (the recipe's author and the issue reporter) closed the MR and redirected to the suggests-only approach in #3487703.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3487703: Bring a few add-on recipes for events into the project template
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 4 files, +58/−17 · json +28/−17 · php +27 · yml +3
  • Discussion: #3487703 · 3 contributors · 16 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

This is a closed proposal (MR !216) that was never merged into Drupal CMS. The intent was to make three contrib event add-ons, drupal/events_calendar, drupal/events_recurring, and drupal/events_registration, more discoverable to site owners by listing them as Composer suggestions in the Events recipe and surfacing them in Project Browser. The MR was ultimately closed without merging: the Drupal CMS Event content type had diverged significantly from the contrib add-ons' content model, breaking compatibility. The project's later shift toward a site-templates architecture removed the motivation to invest further in this direction. Site owners seeking these capabilities can still use the contrib recipes independently in a custom site template.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The MR made four changes. First, drupal_cms_events/composer.json gained a suggest block listing drupal/events_calendar, drupal/events_recurring, and drupal/events_registration, plus a conflict entry for drupal/events < 1.1.1 to prevent applying older contrib Events recipes that conflict with the Drupal CMS version. Second, project_template/composer.json added all three as hard require entries alongside the existing Drupal CMS components. Third, the starter-recipe Project Browser configuration added the three package names to enabled_sources. Fourth, ComponentValidationTest::testSuggestedRecipes() was added to dynamically read the suggest keys from composer.json at test time, look up each installed path via Composer\InstalledVersions::getInstallPath(), and apply each recipe twice to verify idempotency, skipping gracefully when a package is not installed.

A blocking technical obstacle emerged during review: the contrib add-ons depend on the standalone drupal/events recipe, which conflicted with the Drupal CMS Events recipe. The proposed workaround was to use strict: false in the contrib recipe (available from Drupal 10.4+ and 11.1+), but that would have required a new minor branch of the upstream contrib package. The conflict was reportedly resolved, but by then the Event content type in Drupal CMS had drifted far enough from the contrib add-ons' data model that full compatibility was no longer assured, and the issue was closed as won't-fix.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: November 15, 2024

Key contributors

  • mandclu (Acquia)
  • phenaproxima (Acquia)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)

Collaboration

The key design decision, whether to bring the add-ons in as Composer dependencies or merge them wholesale into Drupal CMS, was worked out openly in the thread. phenaproxima framed the two options clearly: keep the three recipes in contrib and pull them in as dependencies (door #1), or fully absorb them into Drupal CMS and deprecate the contrib projects. pameeela confirmed that surfacing add-ons in a recipes UI was the intended UX. mandclu chose door #1. phenaproxima then unblocked the MR after mandclu fixed bugs in the upstream contrib Events recipe. The blocking compatibility issue with strict: false was identified and explained in detail by phenaproxima. The eventual closure came from the same contributor, who noted that the strategic shift to site templates made the original goal achievable through a different path without further investment in this issue.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3481715: Target Drupal 11.1 for stable release without core patches applied
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Critical unknown (0)
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3481715 · 3 contributors · 7 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

Drupal CMS's stable release was planned to target Drupal 11.1 with no core patches applied, because the automatic_updates module (a key Drupal CMS feature) cannot coexist with cweagans/composer-patches in production builds. Including patches silently disables automatic updates for site owners who have no knowledge of, or ability to manage, the underlying patching. The resolution was to eliminate all core patches from stable Drupal CMS builds before the 11.1 release, which was confirmed complete. The follow-on issue #3488457 tracks moving composer-patches to a dev dependency so it remains available for development but is never present in a deployed stable site.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The package_manager module's ComposerPatchesValidator enforces that cweagans/composer-patches, if installed, must have been explicitly added as a direct dependency by a technical user at the command line. Because Drupal CMS's project template previously pulled in composer-patches automatically (and applied patches inside recipe definitions and drupal_cms_patches/composer.json), the validator would either block updates outright or silently leave patches that could fail to apply after a core update. Even in cases where the validator passed, any patch that no longer applied after an upstream fix would break subsequent automatic updates with no path to recovery for a non-technical site owner. Removing core patches from all stable builds, and demoting composer-patches to a dev-only dependency, resolves the conflict and allows automatic_updates to function as designed for Drupal CMS's target audience.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 18, 2024

Collaboration

catch opened the issue after discussing the blocker with phenaproxima, whose confirmation that webform was now 11.x-compatible cleared the path to starting the 11.x port. A week later, catch updated the summary to reflect the actual state of the codebase after further discussion with phenaproxima. phenaproxima then confirmed in the thread that core was no longer being patched (with one internal-development-only exception that is not applied in real builds). pameeela closed the issue once Drupal CMS had moved to 11.1, redirecting the remaining patches concern to #3488457.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3476802: User Experience (UX) Review of the SEO Dashboard
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Discussion: #3476802 · 2 contributors · 4 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

The SEO Dashboard, added to Drupal CMS for DrupalCon Barcelona, has been removed from the Advanced SEO Recipe (drupal_cms_seo_tools). A UX review concluded that the dashboard delivered a list of links rather than meaningful actions, and that most of its content is already accessible under the "Search and metadata" section of the admin navigation. Site owners who had this recipe applied will no longer see a dedicated SEO tab in the Dashboard, and the shortcut to /admin/dashboard/seo is gone.

Impact

Affects front-end developers.

  • Theme change: The SEO Dashboard tab at /admin/dashboard/seo has been removed; any navigation or shortcuts pointing to that path will break.

Technical details

The removal was implemented in commit 3322115 (issue #3488285). Three things were deleted from the drupal_cms_seo_tools recipe: the dashboard configuration file (dashboard.dashboard.seo.yml), the shortcut config entity (shortcut.shortcut.seo_dashboard.yml), and the dashboard module from the recipe's install list. The drupal/dashboard: ^2 Composer dependency was also dropped from the recipe's composer.json. The prerequisite recipe core/recipes/full_html_format_editor, which had been included solely to support the dashboard's text blocks, was likewise removed. The recipe description was updated to drop the reference to the dashboard.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 25, 2024

Key contributors

  • thejimbirch (Kanopi Studios)
  • pameeela (Technocrat)

Collaboration

pameeela provided a detailed annotated screenshot and written analysis of the dashboard's weaknesses: the primary CTA for adding URL aliases was rarely needed, "Fix broken links" was misleading because the target was a 404 report rather than a link checker, and the external resources section did not justify a dedicated dashboard. The conclusion in her comment was "what if we move the common 'jobs to be done' links into the main dashboard?" This analysis directly motivated the decision to remove the dashboard rather than revise it.

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3479721: Create placeholder blocks for welcome dashboard
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 2 files, +82/−4 · yml +82/−4
  • Discussion: #3479721 · 3 contributors · 10 comments
  • Followers: 4

Summary

This proposal populates the Drupal CMS welcome dashboard with a set of default blocks instead of a nearly empty screen, giving site owners something useful to look at right after installation. The MR was ultimately closed by the contributor themselves as "outdated after the new designs," so the specific layout described here was not merged. The issue is marked Fixed, meaning the goal of a populated welcome dashboard was achieved through other means.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The diff modifies the welcome dashboard config YAML (inside the drupal_cms_content_studio recipe area) and its feature definition. The layout is restructured from a single layout_onecol section to a layout_twocol_section (75-25 column split) followed by a second layout_onecol section for a content listing.

In the two-column section, the first column receives: a renamed "Set up analytics" menu block (previously labeled "Welcome"), an "Add new capabilities" dashboard_text_block placeholder, a "Site Analytics" dashboard_text_block placeholder (body: "Coming soon..."), and a dashboard_site_status block. The second column receives a dashboard_text_block containing raw HTML with primary-button links to /node/add/page, /node/add/event, and /node/add/blog. A @todo comment (referencing #3481417) flags those CTA buttons as needing a better UX approach. The new one-column section adds a views_block:dashboard_content-block_site_content block to list recent site content.

The feature definition is also updated to include a config: import: dashboard: '*' directive, pulling in all config provided by the dashboard module.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: October 9, 2024

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.

item.title: #3476543: Create a FrankenPHP-based launcher so that people who don't have DDEV can run Drupal CMS locally
item.description:
• Project: Drupal CMS
  • Type: Normal feature request
  • Status: Fixed
  • Changes: 5 files, +80/−9 · command +76 · json +2/−8 · yml +1/−1 · gitignore +1
  • Discussion: #3476543 · 2 contributors · 7 comments
  • Followers: 8

Summary

This proposal adds a Launch Drupal CMS.command shell script to the Drupal CMS project template, giving users a way to run Drupal CMS locally without installing DDEV, Docker, PHP, or Composer. Double-clicking the script (on macOS; also works on Linux from the terminal) downloads FrankenPHP and Composer binaries on first run, installs Composer dependencies, starts the FrankenPHP application server, and opens the Drupal installer in a browser. The MR was closed without merging after internal discussion: phenaproxima concluded that rolling a custom launcher would be painful to maintain, and that an existing third-party tool should be officially adopted instead. The approach was left as a proof of concept; the policy discussion continues in #3476832.

Impact

No upgrade or configuration changes required.

Technical details

The script (project_template/Launch Drupal CMS.command) is a POSIX shell script placed at the project root. On first run it creates a bin/ directory (added to .gitignore), downloads the appropriate FrankenPHP standalone binary from GitHub releases (v1.2.5, detected via uname for macOS or Linux) and the Composer PHAR (2.7.9), then runs $FRANKENPHP php-cli $COMPOSER install to install dependencies when no composer.lock is present. It then downloads a Caddyfile from Drupal core's repository into the web/ directory (scaffolded via a patch for #3437187) and starts FrankenPHP with frankenphp run --pidfile=.... On macOS it auto-quits Terminal when the script exits via osascript. Subsequent runs reuse the already-downloaded binaries. A PID file guards against double-starts. The existing composer launch script (which called quick-start drupal_cms_installer) is removed from composer.json and replaced by instructions to use the new .command file. The .ddev/config.yaml hook is also updated to check for composer.lock rather than the vendor directory as the signal that dependencies are installed.

Contribution

Timeline

  • Opened: September 24, 2024

Collaboration

phenaproxima opened the MR and tested it successfully on macOS, noting that macOS quarantine required a manual one-time approval step. After discussion with tim.plunkett and effulgentsia, phenaproxima closed the MR without merging, framing it as a proof of concept and redirecting the conversation to the broader policy question of which non-Docker runtime Drupal CMS should officially adopt (#3476832).

This content is AI-generated and may contain errors. See Drupal Digests for more.
